stories.headline: Colorado lawmakers introduce sweeping AI regulations overhaul in final stretch of legislative session
stories.summary:
Colorado lawmakers introduced Senate Bill 189 on Friday, representing the state's third attempt in 12 months to rewrite beleaguered artificial intelligence regulations originally passed in 2024 but never implemented. The bill would require companies to disclose when AI is used to make consequential decisions — such as hiring or financial lending — and give consumers the right to request information about those decisions and correct data involved in them.

Senate Majority Leader Robert Rodriguez, the Denver Democrat who authored the original regulations, is sponsoring the new bill. It is the product of a Governor Polis-created task force that brought together tech groups, industries, consumer protection organizations and others who had previously been at odds over the rules.

Previous rewrite attempts failed in the 2025 legislative session and during a special session in August, with the latter collapsing after business and tech groups opposed a negotiated deal. If lawmakers do not pass a rewrite before the session ends May 13, the original — and widely criticized — regulations will take effect next month.

Key unresolved questions in prior debates centered on liability: who bears responsibility when an AI system discriminates against someone. Under SB-189, liability would fall on either the AI developer or the deployer depending on the circumstances.

stories.headline: Pro-union Worker Protection Act passes Colorado legislature, heads toward expected Polis veto
stories.summary:
The Colorado legislature passed House Bill 26-1005, dubbed the Worker Protection Act by supporters, along party lines Friday, sending it to Governor Jared Polis, who is widely expected to veto the measure for the second consecutive year. The bill would repeal an 80-year-old Colorado law requiring workers to hold a second election — requiring a three-quarter majority — before a union can negotiate over union security, a rule supporters say is uniquely burdensome and designed to impede organizing.

Polis's office confirmed the governor's opposition, stating the legislation is essentially unchanged from the version he vetoed last year and that no durable agreement between labor and business was reached. "The governor clearly outlined last year that he needed to see a durable and lasting agreement between labor and business on this legislation and that did not happen," spokesperson Eric Maruyama said.

The bill is a top priority for legislative Democrats and state labor unions, while the Colorado Chamber of Commerce and Republican lawmakers oppose it. Supporters argue the second-election requirement gives employers additional time to pressure workers and delay negotiations after an initial unionization vote.

Because Polis is in his final year as governor, bill sponsors say they plan to look to his successor to advance the policy if it is vetoed again, marking a shift in their long-term strategy.

stories.headline: Colorado legislature passes $48.6 billion state budget with Medicaid cuts, sends to governor
stories.summary:
The Colorado legislature approved a $48.6 billion state budget and transmitted it to Governor Jared Polis, capping a difficult budget cycle marked by significant cuts to Medicaid and other state programs. The budget reflects the constraints of a revenue environment that forced lawmakers to make hard choices across multiple departments.

Medicaid reductions were among the most contentious elements of the final spending plan, with advocates warning the cuts could limit healthcare access for low-income Coloradans. Legislative budget writers argued the reductions were necessary to keep the state's finances balanced under statutory spending limits.

The budget passage marks a key milestone in the legislative session, which is set to conclude May 13. It now awaits action from Polis, who has the authority to line-item veto specific appropriations.

stories.headline: Colorado wildfire outlook for 2026 raises alarm for Front Range and Western Colorado this summer
stories.summary:
Governor Jared Polis declared May as Wildfire Awareness Month and joined state fire officials Thursday to present a concerning summer wildfire outlook, warning of significantly elevated fire risk in June and July, particularly along the Front Range and in Western Colorado. Polis cited drought conditions affecting 97 percent of the state and high winds as the primary drivers of increased danger.

The state's Division of Fire Prevention and Control said Colorado has roughly 12,000 firefighters statewide and emphasized the importance of interagency coordination, noting resources will be stretched thin. Officials highlighted aggressive initial attack as an effective strategy developed over the past decade.

Polis noted that while fire preparedness has improved — with more aircraft, air tankers and satellite intelligence — the risk has grown alongside it, in part because more people are living in the wildland-urban interface. He urged residents to dispose of cigarettes properly, follow fire restrictions and remove flammable materials near homes, adding that mitigation efforts could result in lower homeowners insurance premiums.

stories.headline: Denver, Aurora water restrictions to remain in place despite spring rain as drought damage runs deep
stories.summary:
Recent spring rainfall across metro Denver has greened lawns temporarily but done little to address the record water deficits driving extreme drought conditions, according to Denver Water officials and National Weather Service meteorologists. Streamflows feeding metro water supplies remain far below normal — at roughly 18 percent of average in the Colorado River Basin and just 2 percent in the South Platte River Basin.

Denver Water's supply manager Nathan Elder said it is "incredibly unlikely" the two-day-a-week outdoor watering restrictions imposed on the 1.5 million residents served by the utility will be lifted at any point this year. "The damage to our water resources has already been done," Elder said, noting that rain does not replenish reservoirs the way spring snowmelt runoff does. Denver Water reservoirs remain mostly below 80 percent capacity.

Aurora Water, with its reservoirs at 57 percent full, is continuing to enforce mandatory two-day-a-week restrictions through a team of six water monitors. The city has issued hundreds of warnings to violators, with fines ranging from $125 to $500 for repeat offenders.

National Weather Service meteorologist Zach Hiris said the metro would need a prolonged period of well-above-average precipitation to see meaningful drought relief. Forecasters expect weekend temperatures to warm into the 70s, with possible showers returning Monday through Wednesday.

stories.headline: Federal charges filed against juvenile in swatting incident that triggered CU Boulder campus lockdown
stories.summary:
Federal authorities have charged a juvenile in connection with a swatting incident last August that sent police, including a SWAT team, to the University of Colorado Boulder's Norlin Library after a fake 911 call reported shots fired on campus. The false report prompted a campus-wide shelter-in-place order, though no shooting occurred.

The juvenile, whose identity was not released by authorities, is described as a self-identified member of the cybercriminal organization known as "Purgatory." The U.S. Department of Justice and CU Boulder said the incident was one of many similar attacks targeting universities across the country, with institutions chosen at random rather than specifically targeted.

The CU Boulder Police Department worked with the FBI to investigate the case. Authorities did not specify the exact charges filed against the juvenile. The perpetrator was never physically present on the Boulder campus.

stories.headline: Denver man pleads guilty to second-degree murder in Jefferson Park shooting, faces up to 38 years
stories.summary:
A 25-year-old Denver man faces up to 38 years in prison after pleading guilty this week to second-degree murder in the 2025 shooting death of another man in Denver's Jefferson Park neighborhood. Rafael Acosta-Hernandez entered his plea Thursday in Denver District Court, according to the Denver District Attorney's office.

On March 6, 2025, Acosta-Hernandez shot and killed 25-year-old Leonel Silva-Perez in the 2600 block of North Zuni Street following a conflict and verbal exchange between the two men. Sentencing has been scheduled for July 10.

stories.headline: Boulder-based Zayo Group closes $4.25 billion fiber network acquisition from Crown Castle
stories.summary:
Zayo Group, the Boulder-based fiber network provider, has completed a $4.25 billion acquisition of fiber assets from Crown Castle, significantly expanding its network capacity to serve AI data centers across North America. The deal represents one of the largest infrastructure transactions involving a Colorado-headquartered company in recent memory.

The acquisition is expected to bolster Zayo's position in the rapidly growing market for high-capacity data connectivity, driven in large part by surging demand from artificial intelligence workloads. Zayo provides fiber and bandwidth infrastructure services to enterprises, carriers and technology companies.

stories.headline: Commerce City targets nearly a dozen large industrial sites for development to fuel economic growth
stories.summary:
The northern Denver suburb of Commerce City says nearly a dozen large industrial sites are shovel-ready and available for immediate construction, as the city positions itself to attract new business and manufacturing investment. The push is part of a broader economic development strategy aimed at diversifying the city's tax base and creating employment opportunities for residents.

The initiative comes as the broader Denver metro industrial real estate market continues to attract logistics, distribution and manufacturing tenants. Commerce City's proximity to Denver International Airport and major freight corridors makes it a competitive location for large-scale industrial users.

stories.headline: Justin Simmons formally retires as a Bronco, ten years after being drafted by Denver
stories.summary:
Former Denver Broncos safety Justin Simmons officially announced his retirement from the NFL on Wednesday at Broncos Park Powered by CommonSpirit, retiring as a member of the organization that drafted him exactly ten years earlier. Simmons, a two-time Pro Bowl and four-time All-Pro selection, spent eight seasons with the Broncos and is widely regarded as one of the best safeties of his era.

Simmons was accompanied by his family for the formal retirement ceremony. His tenure in Denver included numerous marquee performances and significant community involvement, cementing his status as one of the more beloved players in the franchise's recent history.

The retirement closes a chapter on one of the Broncos' most decorated defensive backs in the post-Super Bowl 50 era. Simmons departed Denver after the 2023 season and spent time with other teams before electing to retire as a Bronco.

stories.headline: Rockies return home to host Atlanta Braves, looking to build on winning road trip
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies open a three-game home series against the Atlanta Braves on Friday night at Coors Field, riding momentum from a winning road trip that saw them sweep the New York Mets and take one of three from the Cincinnati Reds. The Rockies reached 14 wins five weeks earlier in 2026 than the historically poor 2025 team managed.

Left-hander Jose Quintana starts for Colorado against Atlanta right-hander Grant Holmes. Quintana earned his first win of the season against the Mets, allowing one run in 5 1/3 innings after a rough outing against the Dodgers. Outfielder Troy Johnston leads the Rockies with a .313 batting average.

The Braves enter with the best record in the majors at 22-10, a seven-win improvement over their disappointing 2025 campaign. First baseman Matt Olson leads Atlanta with nine home runs, 13 doubles and 28 RBIs. The series begins the first leg of a nine-game road trip for the Braves that also takes them to Seattle and Los Angeles.

stories.headline: Nuggets' playoff exit prompts calls for sweeping changes to roster, coaching staff
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets' first-round playoff elimination at the hands of the Minnesota Timberwolves — who were missing three of their top players — has prompted sharp scrutiny of the franchise's direction, with analysts and commentators calling for significant changes from coaching to roster construction. Denver lost Game 6 at Target Center 110-98 to fall 4-2 in the series, with three of those losses coming in Minnesota where the Nuggets failed to reach 100 points.

Nikola Jokic had a subpar series by his standards, as did guard Jamal Murray, who shot 4-for-17 in the clinching game. Critics noted the Nuggets were outcoached by Minnesota's Chris Finch and were outrebounded badly throughout the series, giving up 19 more field goal attempts than the Wolves over the course of the series.

Among the moves being discussed are potential trades of veteran players, a review of coach David Adelman's future with the team and the broader question of whether the franchise's championship window has closed. Jokic has indicated he still wants to remain a Nugget long-term, and an extension for the two-time champion is widely expected.

The loss is being characterized as the most humiliating playoff exit in the Jokic era, with the team having entered as a legitimate championship contender. Front office decisions this offseason, including possible trades and coaching changes, will shape whether the franchise can retool around its franchise center.

joke.joke: You know it's a good hotel when they put a chocolate on your pillow. You know it's a bad hotel when there's a mint on the toilet.

topStory.headline: Federal Appeals Court Blocks Mailing of Abortion Pill Mifepristone, Requiring In-Person Dispensing Nationwide
topStory.summary:
A three-judge panel of the New Orleans-based 5th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals unanimously ruled Friday to reinstate a requirement that mifepristone, one of two drugs used in medication abortions, be dispensed only in person at clinics or medical offices. The decision overrides a 2023 Food and Drug Administration regulation that had permanently allowed the pill to be prescribed via telehealth and mailed directly to patients. The ruling stems from a lawsuit brought by the state of Louisiana and will remain in effect as that case continues through the courts.

The panel, composed of judges nominated by Republican presidents, sided with Louisiana's argument that allowing mifepristone to be mailed into the state undermines its near-total ban on abortion. Circuit Judge Stuart Kyle Duncan wrote that every abortion facilitated by the FDA's mailing regulation "cancels Louisiana's ban on medical abortions" and conflicts with the state's legal position that life begins at conception. Louisiana Attorney General Liz Murrill celebrated the ruling, while the American Civil Liberties Union's Julia Kaye called the decision one that "defies clear science and settled law."

Mifepristone was first approved by the FDA in 2000 and is typically used alongside a second drug, misoprostol, to end early pregnancies. The FDA originally required in-person dispensing, but that restriction was lifted temporarily during the Covid-19 pandemic under President Biden and made permanent in 2023. Surveys indicate that medication abortion now accounts for more than half of all abortions in the United States, and approximately one in four abortions nationally are prescribed through telehealth.

On Friday night, Danco Laboratories, the manufacturer of mifepristone, filed for an emergency one-week stay of the court's order and said it would appeal to the U.S. Supreme Court if the appellate court does not grant relief. Danco's lawyers warned the ruling created "immediate chaos," leaving it unclear whether pharmacies could continue dispensing the drug. The FDA under the Trump administration has said it is conducting its own safety review of mifepristone, though the agency has not indicated when that review will be complete.

The ruling is considered the most significant blow to abortion access in the United States since the Supreme Court overturned Roe v. Wade in 2022. The impact is expected to fall most heavily on people in states where abortion is banned who have relied on telehealth prescriptions and mail delivery to obtain the medication. The case is widely expected to reach the Supreme Court, which in 2024 unanimously rejected a separate challenge to mifepristone's FDA approval but left open the possibility of future legal challenges to the drug's distribution rules.
